note_data.title: Chemical analysis
keyPoints: Pure substances have fixed composition, fixed melting/boiling point, produce only 1 spot on chromatogram, and all molecules are the same
Mixtures have variable composition, variable boiling/melting point, produce 2 or more spots on chromatogram, and contain 2 or more different molecules
Compounds are 2 or more elements chemically joined together and contain covalent or ionic bonds
Mixtures are 2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together
Filtration separates insoluble solid from liquid - liquid particles are small enough to go through filter paper pores while solid particles are too large
Crystallization is used for obtaining solid from solid and liquid solution by forming crystals from a cooling liquid or saturated solution
Distillation is used for obtaining pure liquid from solution - pure liquid has low boiling point and changes to gas easily while solid has high boiling point
Fractional distillation separates 2 liquids which are mixed (miscible) - the liquid with lowest boiling point comes out first at the top of fractionating column
Chromatography is used for separating mixture of organic compounds such as color dyes
The solid obtained in filtration is called 'Residue' and the liquid obtained is called 'Filtrate'

definitions.definition: 2 or more elements chemically joined together
2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together
A solution where no more solid can dissolve in it
Formation of crystals from a cooling liquid/saturated solution OR obtaining solid from solid and liquid solution

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give the method step by step using command words like 'add', 'heat', 'observe'
Show all working clearly and give the final answer with correct units

modelAnswer.question: Describe how to carry out a flame test to identify the metal cation in a salt sample.
modelAnswer.answer: Dip a clean nichrome wire loop into concentrated hydrochloric acid. Pick up a small amount of the salt sample on the wire loop. Hold the wire loop in the blue flame of a Bunsen burner and observe the flame colour.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Clean the wire loop with concentrated hydrochloric acid
Mark 2: Pick up sample on the wire loop
Mark 3: Heat in Bunsen flame and observe colour

commonMistakes: Confusing whether a substance is pure or a mixture - check if it has fixed or variable boiling/melting point
Not understanding that crystallization is always the method to obtain crystals from solutions
Mixing up filtrate and residue - filtrate is the liquid that passes through, residue is the solid that remains
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain crystals, the process will be 'Crystallization'
The constant temperature during distillation equals the boiling point of the pure liquid
formulas: volume = cm³ ÷ 1000 to convert to dm³
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Titration apparatus showing burette clamped above conical flask containing alkali and indicator, with white tile underneath to see colour change clearly
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe flame tests for sodium, potassium and copper ions, including expected observations and safety precautions
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on identifying the correct precipitate color when testing for iron(II) ions with sodium hydroxide

This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — comprehensive question requiring students to plan tests to identify unknown metal ions and anions in a mixture, including reagents and observations
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P3 Q2 (5 marks) — practical question asking students to identify three unknown solutions using chemical tests and record observations in a table

workedExample.question: A student has three unknown solutions A, B and C. Describe chemical tests to identify whether each solution contains iron(II) ions, iron(III) ions, or copper(II) ions. Include reagents, observations and conclusions. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Add a few drops of sodium hydroxide solution to each test tube containing solutions A, B and C
Step 2: Observe and record the color of any precipitates formed
Step 3: If green precipitate forms → iron(II) ions present
Step 4: If orange/brown precipitate forms → iron(III) ions present
Step 5: If blue precipitate forms → copper(II) ions present
workedExample.answer: Add sodium hydroxide solution to each solution. Iron(II) ions produce a green precipitate: Fe²⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂. Iron(III) ions produce an orange/brown precipitate: Fe³⁺ + 3OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₃. Copper(II) ions produce a blue precipitate: Cu²⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Cu(OH)₂.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct reagent (NaOH), 3 marks for correct observations (green, orange/brown, blue), 2 marks for linking observations to correct ions

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the colors of iron(II) and iron(III) hydroxide precipitates' — always remember iron(II) gives GREEN precipitate, iron(III) gives ORANGE/BROWN precipitate with NaOH

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to mention the need for dilute acid when testing for sulfate ions' — always state that barium chloride test requires dilute hydrochloric acid to eliminate false positives from carbonates
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often described flame colors incorrectly or too vaguely' — be specific: sodium gives YELLOW flame, potassium gives LILAC flame, copper gives BLUE-GREEN flame
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a test tube rack showing the precipitation test setup: 3-4 test tubes in a rack, each containing ~2cm depth of test solution, with a dropper bottle adding reagent (labeled as 'NaOH solution' or 'BaCl₂ + HCl'), and visible colored precipitates at the bottom of tubes. Include labels for precipitate colors (green, orange/brown, blue, white) and safety equipment (goggles, gloves). Also show the flame test setup: Bunsen burner with blue flame, nichrome wire being held in flame, and colored flame emission clearly illustrated.
note_data.quickRecap: Chemical analysis involves distinguishing between pure substances and mixtures using their different properties. Pure substances have fixed compositions and melting/boiling points, while mixtures are variable. Different separation techniques are used based on the nature of the mixture: filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain pure solids, distillation for pure liquids, fractional distillation for miscible liquids, and chromatography for organic compounds.
